# Chapter 3.2 — Plan Stakeholder Engagement (BABOK v3)

> Source: **BABOK® Guide v3**, Chapter 3 *Business Analysis Planning and Monitoring*, §3.2 *Plan Stakeholder Engagement* (pp. 31–36).
> Cleaned, structure-preserving extract for the `babok-v3-software-requirements` skill.

## 3.2.1 Purpose

Plan an approach for **establishing and maintaining effective working relationships** with the stakeholders.

## 3.2.2 Description

A thorough stakeholder analysis identifies all involved stakeholders and analyzes their characteristics. The results define the best collaboration and communication approaches and inform stakeholder-related risk planning.

Complexity grows disproportionately with the number of stakeholders: techniques that work for a handful may break down when engagement moves to dozens, hundreds, or thousands of people.

## 3.2.4 Elements

### .1 Perform Stakeholder Analysis

Stakeholder analysis is performed **repeatedly** as the work continues. A detailed, up-to-date stakeholder list prevents the most damaging elicitation failure: discovering a critical stakeholder late, then having to rework completed analysis.

Sources for identifying stakeholders:

- internal: organisational chart, business processes, the **sponsor**;
- external: existing contracts, anticipated vendors, regulatory and governing bodies, shareholders, customers, suppliers.

For each stakeholder, analyse:

#### Roles

Identify the role(s) each stakeholder plays and how they will contribute to the initiative.

#### Attitudes

Stakeholder attitudes about each of the following can positively or negatively impact a change. Analyse attitudes toward:

- business goals, objectives of the initiative, and proposed solutions;
- business analysis in general;
- level of interest in the change;
- the sponsor;
- team members and other stakeholders;
- collaboration and a team-based approach.

A stakeholder serving in a key role but viewing the change negatively requires collaboration approaches that increase their cooperation.

#### Decision-Making Authority

What can each stakeholder approve / reject? Knowing this up front avoids confusion later and ensures the BA collaborates with the right people for decisions.

#### Level of Power or Influence

Both **formal** (organisational chart) and **informal** influence channels matter. Evaluate whether the influence available to support the change matches the influence needed; if not, develop risk responses and influence strategies (champion building, executive sponsorship, communications).

### .2 Define Stakeholder Collaboration

Plan how each stakeholder will collaborate. Considerations:

- timing and frequency;
- location;
- available tools (wikis, online communities);
- delivery method (in-person, virtual);
- preferences of the stakeholders.

Document as a **stakeholder collaboration plan**; revisit as conditions change.

### .3 Stakeholder Communication Needs

For each stakeholder / group, decide:

- what needs to be communicated;
- delivery method (written / verbal);
- audience;
- when to communicate;
- frequency;
- geographic distribution;
- level of detail;
- level of formality.

Document as a **stakeholder communication plan**; review with stakeholders to ensure expectations are met.

## 3.2.6 Techniques commonly used during stakeholder planning

| Technique | Used to… |
|---|---|
| Brainstorming | produce the initial stakeholder list and identify roles & responsibilities |
| Business Rules Analysis | identify stakeholders who were the source of business rules |
| Document Analysis | review existing organisational assets to support stakeholder planning |
| Interviews | gain deeper information about a specific stakeholder or group |
| Lessons Learned | identify the enterprise's previous experience (success & challenges) with stakeholder engagement |
| Mind Mapping | identify potential stakeholders and the relationships between them |
| Organizational Modelling | identify stakeholders affected by the change via the org structure |
| Process Modelling | categorise stakeholders by the systems / processes that support their work |
| Risk Analysis & Management | identify risks from stakeholder attitudes or unavailability |
| Scope Modelling | show stakeholders outside the solution scope but interacting with it |
| Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas | depict relationships of stakeholders to the solution and to one another |
| Survey or Questionnaire | identify shared characteristics of a stakeholder group |
| Workshops | interact with groups of stakeholders to gain richer information |

## 3.2.8 Output

**Stakeholder Engagement Approach** — list of stakeholders, analysed characteristics, listing of roles and responsibilities for the change, and identification of the collaboration and communication approaches the analyst will use during the initiative.
